cards.short: Yours alone. Nobody reads it; you never have to share it.
cards.detail:
Not “Dear diary” private — private in that you write what you want, nobody looks, and you don’t share any of it. Empty your thoughts onto the page. We’ll mostly do this at the start of class to set aside whatever’s on your mind so you can think.

The point
Some thinking only happens with a pen in your hand. Private writing gives that thinking somewhere to go, with zero stakes.

cards.short: A prompt to respond to — free, but with a direction.
cards.detail:
I (or a classmate) give you a prompt, and you respond. It’s free in that you take it wherever you want; it’s focused in that you start from some guidance. This is where a lot of our thinking-on-paper happens before we shape anything.

The rule
Keep the pen moving. Even when you drift from the prompt, keep going — the drift is often where the good stuff is.

cards.short: Writing about your own thinking — debriefing.
cards.detail:
Thinking about your thinking. I might ask how that last activity went, or what part of today confused you. It feels odd at first, but noticing how you think makes you better at it.

Why bother
You’ll build a written record of what you were thinking when you thought it. That record makes the high-stakes writing — essays — much easier later. I promise.

cards.short: A one-sentence description of the whole text.
cards.detail:
When you look at a painting, you step back until it comes into focus. The overview is that step back: squint, take in the whole, and give a general impression. If you can give the feeling of a piece — or tell its story — you can write an overview.

You don’t have to figure out what it means. You simply describe what’s there.

Keep it quick
No need to be complete or profound. One sentence, general impression. You’ll come back and sharpen it later.

cards.short: A messy list of thoughts about the text.
cards.detail:
The opposite move: get up close and examine. Jot down first thoughts, keep the pen moving, and feel free to write questions and vent frustrations as you go. Your list may seem incoherent — don’t worry.

She’s wearing mismatched clothes.
Her body faces the camera, but her eyes dart back at her friends.
Are they fooling around in a schoolyard?
Trust it
What you notice will have significance — almost always. You’ll have time to return to your first thoughts and keep thinking.

cards.short: First notice; then extend the noticing into inference.
cards.detail:
Stage 1 is raw: a quick overview, and an inventory of whatever you notice — observations and questions, no pressure.

Stage 2 extends your thinking. You revisit an observation and ask, so what? — making an inference. Or you take a conclusion you already jumped to and backtrack to find the detail that led you there. The goal of Stage 2 is to state how a particular observation leads you to a particular idea.

Order is yours
Some people inventory before they overview. Experiment — see which gets your thinking moving.

cards.short: A debatable claim about a text, backed by evidence and reasoning.
cards.detail:
A literary argument is a debatable claim about how a text works or what it means, backed up with evidence from the text and clear reasoning that explains the connection.

“Debatable” is the key word. “The poem has four stanzas” isn’t an argument — nobody disagrees. “The poem argues that beautiful things are temporary” is: someone could read it differently, so you have to make the case.

The three parts
Claim (what you think) · Evidence (what in the text shows it) · Reasoning (why the evidence proves the claim). Missing any one, and it isn’t yet an argument.

cards.short: A group of sentences developing one clear main idea.
cards.detail:
A paragraph is a group of sentences that work together to develop one clear main idea. It starts on a new line (usually indented), often begins with a topic sentence, adds supporting details, and may end with a brief wrap-up.

One idea
“One clear main idea” is the whole job. The moment a paragraph is trying to do two things, it usually wants to be two paragraphs.

cards.short: The single sentence that encapsulates the paragraph's one idea.
cards.detail:
A topic sentence is a single sentence that encapsulates that one clear idea — the paragraph’s argument, stated up front so the reader knows where you’re taking them.

In an analytical paragraph, your topic sentence is your claim.
Robert Frost’s “Nothing Gold Can Stay” argues that the most beautiful things in life are temporary, so we should notice them while they last.
Where it comes from
You don’t start here. The topic sentence is what your noticing becomes once you ask, “so what does all this add up to?”

cards.short: The quotation, plus the sentence that explains why it proves your claim.
cards.detail:
Evidence is the quoted or specific detail from the text. Reasoning is the part students most often skip: the sentence that explains the connection between that evidence and your claim.

Then “leaf subsides to leaf,” showing how quickly that early beauty fades.
Evidence in gold; reasoning in teal. The reasoning is doing the real work — it turns a quotation into proof.

The test
After every quote, ask yourself: so what? how does this show my claim? Your answer is the reasoning. If you can’t answer, the evidence may not fit the claim.

cards.short: A narrative moment, not a whole story. One or two paragraphs.
cards.detail:
We’re usually working in one or two paragraphs — so this is a narrative moment, not a full life story. A single scene, closely observed, is worth more than a summary of a whole year.

Zoom in
Pick the smallest true thing that still matters: the ten seconds before, the one object, the exact sentence someone said. Small and specific beats big and general.

cards.short: Something happens and changes — not a static picture.
cards.detail:
Describing paints a picture that just sits there. Storytelling moves: something happens, and by the end something is different — even if the change is only in how you understand the moment.

The difference
Description: “The kitchen was warm and smelled like bread.” Story: “I came in cold and angry, and the smell of bread caught me before I could keep being angry.” The second one goes somewhere.

cards.short: Give the reader the evidence; let them feel the conclusion.
cards.detail:
“Telling” hands the reader your conclusion (she was nervous). “Showing” gives them what you saw and lets them arrive at it themselves (she kept re-reading the same line, and her pen had chewed-through teeth marks).

Showing trusts the reader. It’s also just more vivid — the reader gets to notice, the way you did.

Not a total ban
You can’t show everything — some telling moves the story along. But when a moment matters, show it. Save telling for the connective tissue.

cards.short: A couple of big things, a few margin notes, a rubric — never an ocean of marks.
cards.detail:
My feedback tries to do two things. First, to tell you I read your work — that I value the time, thought, effort, and vulnerability in it, and that your writing is part of a human-scaled conversation, not a form being processed.

Second, to give you one or two big things to focus on for next time (or if you revise), plus a handful of marginal comments and a rubric. That’s it. I won’t bury you in marks — research is clear that overloaded feedback doesn’t get used.

If it’s not clear
Let’s have a writing conference. A five-minute conversation beats a page of comments neither of us enjoys.

cards.term: Peer Review — Your Job as the Writer
cards.short: Come prepared, and ask for exactly the feedback you want.
cards.detail:
For longer pieces we’ll do brief, focused peer sessions. They only work if you come prepared — with the excerpt you want eyes on.

Then ask specifically where you want feedback: “Does the second sentence land?” “Can you tell what I’m arguing?” Your classmate is a reader — someone who can see what you can’t see yourself. Their reaction is one honest data point, not a verdict.

Prepare an excerpt
Don’t hand over the whole draft and say “thoughts?” Choose the part you’re unsure about and the question you actually want answered.

cards.term: Peer Review — Your Job as the Reader
cards.short: Be the reader you'd want: rooted, generous, and honest.
cards.detail:
Be the reader you wish your classmate to be. Root your observations in the assignment and what we’ve talked about — not in vague taste. Be generous, but honest.

Not sharing something you notice that could help does your classmate a disservice. You can be kind and honest at the same time — those aren’t in tension. Point to what’s working as specifically as what isn’t.

The move
“Here’s what I noticed as a reader: I got a little lost right here, because…” — a reader reporting an experience, not a judge issuing a grade.
